# Submission

This chapter covers how to submit your work to Gradescope without
losing points to formatting. The same workflow applies to all three
project artifacts you submit: the **Pre-project**, the optional
**Project Progress Check**, and the **Final Project**.

In the past four years, the vast majority of pre-projects, project
progress checks, and final projects that earned a 0 were the result
of something done wrong here.

## Filename conventions

Each of the three project artifacts has a required filename. Use
**lowercase** for first and last name, **underscores** between
words, and the **`.pdf`** extension.

| Artifact | Filename pattern | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-project | `firstname_lastname_pre.pdf` | `jane_doe_pre.pdf` |
| Project Progress Check | `firstname_lastname_progress.pdf` | `jane_doe_progress.pdf` |
| Final Project | `firstname_lastname_project.pdf` | `jane_doe_project.pdf` |

::: {.callout-important}
Use the filename pattern exactly as written. It keeps your three
submissions distinct in your project folder (so a later one
doesn't overwrite an earlier one), and it makes each artifact easy
to locate when you need to refer back to it. Required, not
optional.
:::

## Late submission policy

Per the syllabus: **late submissions will not be accepted** for the
Pre-project, the Project Progress Check, or the Final Project. The
relevant deadlines are on the course schedule. There are no extensions
and no late-credit options.

## The submission workflow at a glance

Each submission goes through the same three steps:

1. **Knit** your `.Rmd` to HTML inside RStudio (the **Knit** button
   in the editor pane).

   ::: {.callout-important}
   Click the **Knit** button itself, not the small dropdown arrow
   next to it. The dropdown offers "Knit to HTML / PDF / Word"
   options that override the output format we've configured in the
   Rmd's YAML — picking one will overwrite our formatting and your
   output will be rejected.
   :::

2. **Save** the resulting HTML page as a PDF using your browser's
   print-to-PDF function (instructions per browser below).
3. **Upload** the PDF to the corresponding Gradescope assignment
   under the correct filename.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Do not knit directly to PDF

Knitting straight to PDF from R Markdown uses a different rendering
path (LaTeX) that strips the CSS-based slide formatting we use. The
output will not match the reference and **will receive a 0%** as a
formatting violation.

You **must** knit to HTML, then save the HTML as PDF.
:::

## Saving HTML as PDF {#saving-html-as-pdf}

After knitting, the HTML output opens in your browser (or in
RStudio's Viewer pane — if it opens there, click the small icon at
the top of the Viewer to open it in your default browser).

In every browser, the print dialog is what you use to save as PDF.
The settings you need are roughly the same across browsers:

- **Destination:** "Save as PDF"
- **Layout / Orientation:** **Landscape**
- **Color:** Color (not Black and White)
- **Margins:** Default or None
- **Pages per sheet:** 1
- **Background graphics:** **Enabled** (this is the easy-to-miss one
  — without it, the colored UGA styling disappears)

Per-browser walkthroughs below.

### Google Chrome (and Microsoft Edge)

Edge uses the same print dialog as Chrome — these steps work for
both.

1. Click the three vertical dots in the top-right of the browser.
2. Click **Print**.
3. Match the settings shown below — most importantly, **Destination
   = Save as PDF**, **Layout = Landscape**, and **More settings →
   Background graphics = checked**.

![Chrome print dialog settings](assets/chrome_print.png){fig-alt="Chrome print dialog with Destination set to Save as PDF, Layout set to Landscape, and Background graphics enabled"}

4. Click **Save**. Use the correct filename when prompted (see
   [Filename conventions](#filename-conventions)).

### Firefox

1. Click the three horizontal lines (the "hamburger" menu) in the
   top-right of the browser.
2. Click **Print**.
3. Match the settings shown below across the two screens. Key
   settings: **Destination = Save to PDF**, **Orientation =
   Landscape**, and **Options → Print backgrounds = checked**.

![Firefox print settings — page 1](assets/firefox_print_1.png){fig-alt="Firefox print dialog with Destination set to Save to PDF and Orientation set to Landscape"}

![Firefox print settings — page 2](assets/firefox_print_2.png){fig-alt="Firefox print dialog showing the Options panel with Print backgrounds enabled"}

4. Click **Save** and use the correct filename.

### Safari

1. Click **File** in the top-left menu bar.
2. Click **Print**.
3. Match the settings shown below across the two screens. Key
   settings: **Orientation = Landscape**, **Print backgrounds =
   checked**, and the **PDF dropdown in the bottom-left** must be
   set to **Save as PDF** (not Print).

![Safari print settings — page 1](assets/safari_print_1.png){fig-alt="Safari print dialog with Orientation set to Landscape and Print backgrounds enabled"}

![Safari print settings — page 2](assets/safari_print_2.png){fig-alt="Safari print dialog with the PDF dropdown set to Save as PDF"}

4. Click **Save** and use the correct filename.

## Pre-project reference: what your three slides should look like

Below are the three pre-project slides rendered correctly. Your
knitted PDF should match these for **formatting** (fonts, colors,
slide layout, code chunk styling). The **content** will differ —
your name will be on the title slide and the numbers in your
print-out and your fill-ins will reflect your `LF.csv`.

::: {.callout-note}
The images below show legacy examples (date stamp from a prior
semester, comma in the name placeholder). They are reference for
the slide *format*, not the exact text content. Your version will
show Summer 2026, your name without a comma, and the current CPS
numbers.
:::

### Reference slide 1 — Title slide

![Title slide reference](assets/ref_slide_1_title.png){fig-alt="Pre-project title slide showing 'BUSN 5000 :: Pre-Project Exercise', subtitle 'Measuring Labor Force Participation', placeholder for student name, and date"}

What yours should look like:

- Title and subtitle as shown
- Your first and last name on the author line
- Summer 2026 date

### Reference slide 2 — Read `LF.csv` and print its contents

![LF print-out slide reference](assets/ref_slide_2_lf.png){fig-alt="Pre-project slide showing the read_csv code chunk and the resulting LF data frame printed in the slide"}

What yours should look like:

- The code chunk visible (formatted with the UGA code-block style)
- The `lf` data frame printed below it with six named columns

### Reference slide 3 — Document the contents of `LF.csv`

![Communication slide reference](assets/ref_slide_3_comm.png){fig-alt="Pre-project slide showing the documentation paragraph about LFPR with all six blanks filled in"}

What yours should look like:

- The paragraph fully filled in, no remaining `_____` blanks
- Values rounded to two decimal places
- Numbers matching your `out/LF.csv` (not numbers copied from this
  reference)

## Final Project reference: what your 35 slides should look like

Below are the 35 final-project slides rendered correctly. Your
knitted PDF should match these for **formatting** (fonts, colors,
slide layout, tables, figures). The **content** will reflect your
own work — your name on the title slide, your written sections,
your data outputs, and your figures.

::: {.callout-note}
The images below show legacy examples (date stamp from a prior
semester). The format is what to match. Your version will show
Summer 2026 and current CPS numbers.
:::

### Front matter (slides 1–2)

![Slide 1: Title](assets/project_slide-01.png){fig-alt="Title slide of the BUSN 5000 final project"}

![Slide 2: Academic Honesty Statement](assets/project_slide-02.png){fig-alt="Academic Honesty Statement slide with signature line"}

### Introduction (slides 3–4)

![Slide 3: Introduction (section divider)](assets/project_slide-03.png){fig-alt="Section divider slide for Introduction"}

![Slide 4: Overview](assets/project_slide-04.png){fig-alt="Project overview slide"}

### Data (slides 5–8)

![Slide 5: Data (section divider)](assets/project_slide-05.png){fig-alt="Section divider slide for Data"}

![Slide 6: March 2024 CPS](assets/project_slide-06.png){fig-alt="March 2024 CPS description slide"}

![Slide 7: March 2024 CPS Extract](assets/project_slide-07.png){fig-alt="March 2024 CPS Extract description slide with read_data code chunk"}

![Slide 8: Analysis sample](assets/project_slide-08.png){fig-alt="Analysis sample slide with btl1 code chunk"}

### Baseline earnings distributions (slides 9–12)

![Slide 9: Baseline earnings distributions (section divider)](assets/project_slide-09.png){fig-alt="Section divider slide for Baseline earnings distributions"}

![Slide 10: Plotting earnings distributions](assets/project_slide-10.png){fig-alt="Plotting earnings distributions slide with btl2 code chunk"}

![Slide 11: Distribution of earnings by gender (Figure 1)](assets/project_slide-11.png){fig-alt="Figure 1: Distribution of earnings by gender"}

![Slide 12: Baseline comparisons](assets/project_slide-12.png){fig-alt="Baseline comparisons write-up slide"}

### The career gender gap (slides 13–20)

![Slide 13: The career gender gap (section divider)](assets/project_slide-13.png){fig-alt="Section divider slide for The career gender gap"}

![Slide 14: Wages and hours differences (Table 1)](assets/project_slide-14.png){fig-alt="Table 1: Wages and hours by gender"}

![Slide 15: Documenting the differences](assets/project_slide-15.png){fig-alt="Documenting the differences write-up slide"}

![Slide 16: Plotting career log wage profiles](assets/project_slide-16.png){fig-alt="Plotting career log wage profiles slide with mi2 code chunk"}

![Slide 17: Career log wage profiles (Figure 2)](assets/project_slide-17.png){fig-alt="Figure 2: Career log-wage profiles for women and men"}

![Slide 18: Estimating wage differences over a career](assets/project_slide-18.png){fig-alt="Estimating wage differences over a career slide with mi3 code chunk"}

![Slide 19: Evolution of the gender wage gap (Table 2)](assets/project_slide-19.png){fig-alt="Table 2: Percent wage differences, first 30 years"}

![Slide 20: Discussing the gender wage gap evolution](assets/project_slide-20.png){fig-alt="Discussing the gender wage gap evolution write-up slide"}

### Explaining the gender wage gap (slides 21–30)

![Slide 21: Explaining the gender wage gap (section divider)](assets/project_slide-21.png){fig-alt="Section divider slide for Explaining the gender wage gap"}

![Slide 22: Fitting the log wage profiles](assets/project_slide-22.png){fig-alt="Fitting the log wage profiles slide with reg1 code chunk"}

![Slide 23: Log wage profiles with quadratic fits (Figure 3)](assets/project_slide-23.png){fig-alt="Figure 3: Career log-wage profiles with quadratic fits"}

![Slide 24: Gender differences in education (Table 3)](assets/project_slide-24.png){fig-alt="Table 3: Educational attainment by gender"}

![Slide 25: Gender differences in demographics (Table 4)](assets/project_slide-25.png){fig-alt="Table 4: Demographic characteristics by gender"}

![Slide 26: Documenting differences in characteristics](assets/project_slide-26.png){fig-alt="Documenting differences in characteristics write-up slide"}

![Slide 27: Controlling for education and demographic characteristics](assets/project_slide-27.png){fig-alt="Controlling for education and demographic characteristics slide with reg2a code chunk"}

![Slide 28: Reporting the results](assets/project_slide-28.png){fig-alt="Reporting the results slide with reg2b code chunk"}

![Slide 29: Explaining the gender wage gap (Table 5)](assets/project_slide-29.png){fig-alt="Table 5: OLS estimates of the gender wage gap"}

![Slide 30: Documenting the findings](assets/project_slide-30.png){fig-alt="Documenting the findings write-up slide"}

### Conclusion (slides 31–32)

![Slide 31: Conclusion (section divider)](assets/project_slide-31.png){fig-alt="Section divider slide for Conclusion"}

![Slide 32: Summary](assets/project_slide-32.png){fig-alt="Summary slide"}

### Appendix (slides 33–35)

![Slide 33: Appendix (section divider)](assets/project_slide-33.png){fig-alt="Section divider slide for Appendix"}

![Slide 34: Data documentation](assets/project_slide-34.png){fig-alt="Data documentation slide with var_doc code chunk"}

![Slide 35: List of main variables with definitions](assets/project_slide-35.png){fig-alt="List of main variables with definitions table"}

## If your output doesn't match the reference

Most formatting mismatches come from a handful of recurring
mistakes. See the
[Common Errors](common-errors.qmd) chapter for the diagnosis and
fix for each — including:

- CSS in the wrong folder
- Wrong orientation (portrait instead of landscape)
- Knit-to-PDF instead of HTML → PDF
- YAML edits that break the rendering
- Background graphics not enabled in the print dialog

If you've checked all of those and still see a difference, email
the teaching team. Attach **both** your `.Rmd` file and the `.pdf`
you generated (the one with the wrong output), and include the
print dialog settings you used.
